title: Findings That Land
subtitle: Rating, reporting & making it useful

hook.metaphor: A doctor who buries 'you have a serious heart condition' on page 40, between 'mild dry skin' and 'slightly low on vitamin D', has failed you — even if every word is technically correct. A great diagnosis is *prioritised and clear*. Audit findings are the same: the value isn't in finding problems, it's in communicating them so the right ones actually get fixed.
hook.scenario: An audit report lists 60 findings, all formatted identically, with the critical fraud-control gap given the same weight as a typo in a procedure document. The executives skim it, fix three easy ones, and miss the big one. Technically thorough — but did it work?

learn: Not all findings are equal, so they must be **rated by severity** — typically high / medium / low (or critical/major/minor). Rating is how you tell the reader 'fix *this* first'. Our report failed precisely because it flattened a critical gap to look like a typo (the materiality lesson, ignored).

A useful finding has a standard anatomy, sometimes called the **'5 Cs'**: **Condition** (what we found), **Criteria** (the standard it breaches), **Cause** (why it happened), **Consequence** (the risk/impact), and **Corrective action** (the recommended fix). 'There's a problem' is useless; this structure makes a finding actionable.

Tone and framing matter. Findings should be **objective and evidence-based**, not personal attacks — and ideally **agreed with management** (who confirm the facts and own a fix with a deadline). An auditor's job is to drive improvement, not to win arguments; a finding nobody accepts rarely gets fixed.

The report should also surface **root cause**, not just symptoms. Ten findings that all stem from one broken process are really *one* problem — fixing the cause beats patching ten symptoms (echoing the people-vs-process lesson from Operational Risk).

And the audience shapes the message: executives and the board need the **critical few** clearly highlighted, not the exhaustive list. Burying the heart condition among the dry skin (our report) is a communication failure that defeats the entire audit.
bridge: So at work, you can judge an audit report by whether it *lands*, not by its page count: *are findings rated so the critical ones stand out? Does each one say what, against what standard, why, the impact, and the fix? Is the root cause named?* A report where the big risk is visible in ten seconds is worth more than a flawless 60-item list nobody acts on.

interactions.title: The flattened report
interactions.prompt: 60 findings, all weighted equally; execs fix three easy ones and miss the critical fraud gap. The failure?

options.label: None — every finding was accurate and included.
options.feedback: Accurate but useless. By giving a critical gap the same weight as a typo, the report failed to communicate priority — and the big risk went unfixed. Thoroughness isn't the goal; impact is.

options.label: Findings weren't rated by severity, so the critical one didn't stand out.
options.feedback: Exactly. Rating and highlighting the critical few is how a report lands. A finding nobody acts on is a finding wasted, however correct.

options.label: They should have added even more findings.
options.feedback: More items in an unprioritised list makes it worse, not better — the critical issue gets buried deeper. Prioritise, don't pile on.

interactions.title: Anatomy of a finding (the 5 Cs)
interactions.prompt: Match each element of a good finding to its meaning.

pairs.right: The standard or rule it breaches
The risk or impact it creates
The recommended fix, with an owner

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “The value of an audit isn't finding problems, it's communicating them — rate findings by severity, give each one the what/standard/why/impact/fix, name the root cause, and make the critical few impossible to miss.”

checkpoint.q: Why must audit findings be rated by severity?
options: So readers know what to fix first and the critical issues stand out
checkpoint.explain: Flattening a critical gap to look like a typo means the big risk gets buried and ignored.

checkpoint.q: A genuinely useful finding includes its…
options: Condition, criteria, cause, consequence, and corrective action
checkpoint.explain: The 5 Cs turn an observation into something actionable that someone can own and fix.

checkpoint.q: Identifying the root cause matters because…
options: Ten symptoms of one broken process are really one problem to fix
checkpoint.explain: Fixing the underlying cause beats patching many symptoms one by one.
